section.title: The Law and the Struggle with Sin
section.sectionRationale: Paul has said the law aroused sinful passions, so he must now defend the law itself. Is the law sin? By no means. The law is holy, righteous, and good; yet sin seized the commandment as its opportunity, so that the very commandment meant for life brought death, and sin was exposed as utterly sinful. Then Paul turns to the searching first person account of the divided self, I do not do the good I want but the evil I do not want, warring with indwelling sin, until the cry, wretched man that I am, who will deliver me, breaks into thanks: thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord.
section.thesis: The law is not sin but holy, righteous, and good; it exposes and provokes sin without power to save, and the honest anatomy of the divided self, delighting in God's law yet held captive to indwelling sin, drives the believer from every hope in self to cry for deliverance and to give thanks to God through Jesus Christ our Lord.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the law is not sin but exposes it; the commandment meant for life brought death, yet the law is holy; the struggle of the divided self with indwelling sin; and the cry of the wretched man answered by thanks to God through Jesus Christ our Lord.

units.label: Is the law sin?
units.note: the law reveals and exposes sin

verses.text: What then shall we say? Is the law sin? Certainly not! Indeed, I would not have been mindful of sin if not for the law. For I would not have been aware of coveting if the law had not said, “Do not covet.”
But {{hamartia|sin}}, seizing its opportunity through the commandment, produced in me every kind of covetous desire. For apart from the law, sin is dead.

units.label: The commandment meant for life brought death
units.note: yet the law is holy, righteous, and good

verses.text: Once I was alive apart from the law; but when the commandment came, sin sprang to life and I died.
So I discovered that the very commandment that was meant to bring life actually brought death.
For sin, seizing its opportunity through the commandment, deceived me and through the commandment put me to death.
So then, the {{nomos|law}} is holy, and the commandment is holy, righteous, and good.

units.label: Sin exposed as utterly sinful
units.note: good turned to death that sin might be seen

verses.text: Did that which is good, then, become death to me? Certainly not! But in order that sin might be exposed as sin, it produced death in me through what was good, so that through the commandment sin might become utterly sinful.

units.label: The divided self
units.note: I do not do the good I want

verses.text: We know that the law is spiritual; but I am unspiritual, sold as a slave to sin.
I do not understand what I do. For what I want to do, I do not do. But what I hate, I do.
And if I do what I do not want to do, I admit that the law is good.
In that case, it is no longer I who do it, but it is sin living in me that does it.
I know that nothing good lives in me, that is, in my {{sarx|flesh}}; for I have the desire to do what is good, but I cannot carry it out.
For I do not do the good I want to do. Instead, I keep on doing the evil I do not want to do.
And if I do what I do not want, it is no longer I who do it, but it is sin living in me that does it.

units.label: Wretched man that I am
units.note: who will deliver me? Thanks be to God

verses.text: So this is the principle I have discovered: When I want to do good, evil is right there with me.
For in my {{eso_anthropos|inner being}} I delight in God’s law.
But I see another law at work in my body, warring against the law of my mind and holding me captive to the law of sin that dwells within me.
What a {{talaiporos|wretched}} man I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death?
Thanks be to God, through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, with my mind I serve the law of God, but with my flesh I serve the law of sin.

nomos.gloss: Law, the law of Moses. Paul defends it emphatically: the law is holy, and the commandment is holy, righteous, and good. The problem is never the law itself but sin, which abuses the good commandment. The law reveals God's will and exposes sin, yet it cannot give the power to obey; it diagnoses the disease it cannot cure.

hamartia.gloss: Sin, here almost personified as an active, deceiving power that seizes its opportunity through the commandment. Sin uses even the good law as a base of operations, provoking the very desire the law forbids. Paul shows sin to be so malignant that it turns the good commandment into an occasion for death, so that sin might be seen as utterly sinful.

sarx.gloss: Flesh, human nature in its fallen weakness and bent toward sin. Paul says nothing good lives in me, that is, in my flesh; the flesh is the foothold of indwelling sin, so that even the desire to do good is frustrated in its carrying out. The struggle of the passage is the struggle against sin still lodged in the flesh.

eso_anthropos.gloss: The inner being or inner man, the deepest self renewed to delight in God. Paul says in my inner being I delight in God's law, describing a genuine, heartfelt love for what God commands. This inward delight, at war with the law of sin in the members, is characteristic of one whose heart has been renewed toward God.

talaiporos.gloss: Wretched, miserable, worn out by struggle. Paul's cry, what a wretched man I am, is the anguished honesty of one who feels the weight of indwelling sin and longs for full deliverance. It is not despair but a groan that gives way at once to thanks to God, who delivers through Jesus Christ our Lord.

basic.context: Paul has just said the law aroused sinful passions, which could make the law sound evil. So he asks, is the law sin? His answer is an emphatic no. The law is good; it shows us what sin is. But sin seizes the good commandment and uses it against us. Then Paul opens up the inner struggle: I want to do good, but I keep doing the evil I hate.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the passage in two parts. First, verses 7 to 13 defend the law and expose sin's malice. Second, verses 14 to 25 give a searching first person account of the divided self at war with indwelling sin. There is a long standing debate about who the I is, but the passage clearly aims to humble every hope in self and to drive us to Christ, ending in thanks to God through Him.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul defends the nomos, the law, as holy and good. The villain is hamartia, sin, an active power that abuses the commandment. Nothing good dwells in the sarx, the flesh, yet in the eso anthropos, the inner being, Paul delights in God's law. The struggle drives him to cry talaiporos, wretched, before bursting into thanks to God through Jesus Christ.
basic.typology: The cry for deliverance from this body of death echoes Israel's groaning in bondage, answered by the exodus, and points forward to the redemption of the body at the resurrection. The good law that could not give life foreshadows the greater work of Christ and the Spirit, who accomplish what the law revealed but could not achieve.
basic.christ: The whole struggle drives to Christ. The question who will rescue me is answered in the next breath: thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord. Only He delivers from the body of death. The law could expose the disease, but Christ alone is the cure, and the chapter's anguished honesty is meant to send us to Him and to the life in the Spirit that Romans 8 unfolds.
basic.god: God gave the law, which is holy, righteous, and good, a reflection of His own character. God is the one to whom Paul gives thanks for deliverance through His Son. The passage guards God's goodness in giving the law while exposing the depth of human sin, so that all the glory of rescue belongs to God alone through Jesus Christ.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8 answers this chapter: what the law could not do, God did by sending His Son and His Spirit. Galatians 5 describes the flesh and the Spirit in conflict. Romans 3 says through the law comes the knowledge of sin. Exodus 20 gives the commandment, Do not covet, that Paul cites.
basic.oneStory: The Bible shows a good law given by a good God, meeting a people unable to keep it, so that sin is exposed and every mouth stopped. This chapter voices that predicament at its most personal, the divided heart crying for rescue. The story does not end in the groan but in the thanks, for God delivers through Jesus Christ our Lord, and Romans 8 opens with no condemnation and life in the Spirit.

context.literary: The chapter divides into a defense of the law, verses 7 to 13, cast in past tense narrative, and the vivid present tense account of the divided self, verses 14 to 25. The repeated pattern, I do not do the good I want but the evil I hate, drives the section, rising to the cry of verse 24 and the thanksgiving of verse 25, which pivots directly into the triumph of chapter 8.
context.historical: Paul writes to a church wrestling with the place of the Mosaic law, and he must clear the law of the charge that it is evil while explaining why it cannot save. The first person singular, unusual in this stretch of argument, personalizes the universal human plight under the law, drawing on the commandment against coveting, the tenth word of the Decalogue, which reaches the heart.
context.canonical: Romans 7 is one of the most debated passages in the New Testament, central to Christian reflection on sin, the law, and the divided self. Augustine, Luther, and Calvin read the struggling I as the regenerate believer, a reading that shaped the Reformation understanding of the Christian's ongoing battle with indwelling sin, answered by the deliverance of Christ and the Spirit in chapter 8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To vindicate the law as holy and good while exposing the deceptive power of indwelling sin, and to lay bare the human inability to keep the law even where the heart delights in it, so that every hope in self collapses and the reader is driven to give thanks for deliverance through Jesus Christ our Lord.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is largely descriptive, an anatomy of sin, the law, and the divided self rather than a set of commands. Its abiding truth is prescriptive in effect: it teaches us to esteem the law as good, to distrust the flesh entirely, and to look for deliverance not to law keeping but to Christ.

debates.question: Who is the I in Romans 7:14 to 25?

views.label: The regenerate believer's ongoing struggle with indwelling sin
views.case: The historic Reformed reading, held by the later Augustine, Luther, and Calvin, notes the present tense, the delight in God's law in the inner being, the hatred of sin, and the will toward good, which fit a renewed heart, not an unbeliever; the passage describes the real and continuing battle of the Christian against sin still lodged in the flesh, a battle answered by the Spirit in chapter 8.

views.label: The unregenerate person, or Paul under the law before Christ
views.case: Others take the I as the person under the law apart from Christ, sold as a slave to sin and unable to do good, arguing that the defeated tone and the captivity to sin do not match the freedom of chapter 6 or the victory of chapter 8; on this view Paul dramatizes life under the law before the Spirit's liberation.

views.label: Israel, or Adam, or a rhetorical everyman under the law
views.case: Some read the I representatively, as Israel meeting the law at Sinai, or Adam meeting the commandment in Eden, or humanity in general under the law, using the first person as a vivid rhetorical device rather than strict autobiography.

debates.bearing: Foregrounding the regenerate reading, the passage gives sober comfort: even the true Christian wages a lifelong war with indwelling sin, yet is not condemned, for deliverance is sure through Jesus Christ and the Spirit; the other readings rightly warn that the law cannot save and that the flesh profits nothing, and all agree the answer is not self effort but Christ.

debates.question: How can the law be good if it brought death?

views.label: The fault lies in sin, not in the law
views.case: The historic reading holds that the law is holy, righteous, and good, and that sin, not the law, produced death by abusing the good commandment; the law rightly exposes and even provokes sin so that sin is seen as utterly sinful.

views.label: The law itself is a defective or enslaving power
views.case: Some speak as though the law itself is the problem to be escaped; but Paul explicitly clears the law of blame, laying the guilt on indwelling sin, so that the law's goodness stands even as it cannot give life.

debates.bearing: Keeping the blame on sin rather than the law honors God, who gave the law, and clarifies the gospel: the law is a good diagnosis that cannot cure, so salvation must come from Christ and the Spirit, not from the law.

study.originalLanguages: Paul labors to defend the nomos, the law, which he calls holy, righteous, and good, a true reflection of God's will. The real agent of ruin is hamartia, sin, portrayed as an active, deceiving power that seizes the good commandment as its base and provokes the very desire it forbids. The struggle centers on two spheres within the person: the sarx, the flesh, in which nothing good dwells and where sin has its foothold, and the eso anthropos, the inner being, which genuinely delights in God's law. Caught between them, Paul cries talaiporos, wretched, a groan of honest anguish that turns at once into thanksgiving to God for deliverance through Jesus Christ our Lord.

typology.type: Bondage and the cry for deliverance
typology.fulfillment: Israel's groaning under slavery, answered by the exodus, foreshadows the cry who will rescue me, answered by redemption in Christ.

typology.type: The good law that cannot give life
typology.fulfillment: The commandment meant for life yet bringing death points forward to Christ and the Spirit, who accomplish what the law could only require.
The longing for rescue from the mortal body anticipates the redemption of the body at the resurrection, secured in the risen Christ.

study.christ: Though the passage dwells on the law and the struggle with sin, it drives relentlessly to Christ. The desperate question, who will rescue me from this body of death, receives its immediate answer: thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord. He alone delivers where the law is powerless; He is the cure for the disease the law only diagnoses. The whole anatomy of the divided self is meant to strip away every confidence in self effort and cast the sinner wholly on Christ, whose deliverance and gift of the Spirit chapter 8 unfolds in full.
study.god: God is the giver of the law, which is holy, righteous, and good, bearing the stamp of His own character, so that the law's goodness is never in doubt. And God is the one to whom Paul gives thanks for deliverance through His Son. The passage upholds the goodness of God in the law while exposing the depth and deceit of human sin, ensuring that every particle of glory in the rescue of sinners belongs to God alone, accomplished through Jesus Christ our Lord.

crossReferences.note: What the law could not do, weakened by the flesh, God did by sending His own Son and His Spirit; no condemnation in Christ.
The flesh and the Spirit are in conflict, so that you do not do what you want, echoing the divided self.
Through the law comes the knowledge of sin, as the commandment made Paul aware of coveting.
Do not covet, the commandment Paul cites, which reaches beyond outward act to the desire of the heart.

study.oneStory: The Bible tells of a good God who gives a good law to a people who cannot keep it, so that sin is exposed and every mouth is stopped before Him. This chapter voices that plight at its most personal: a heart that delights in God's law yet finds sin still warring within, crying out for rescue. The story does not end in the groan but in the thanksgiving, for God delivers through Jesus Christ our Lord, and the next chapter opens with no condemnation and the liberating life of the Spirit.

quiz.q: How does Paul answer the question, is the law sin?

options.text: Certainly not; the law is holy, righteous, and good, and it exposes sin.
options.note: Paul clears the law of blame and lays the fault on sin.

options.text: Yes, the law is itself a sinful and enslaving power.
options.note: Paul emphatically denies this; the law is holy and good.

options.text: The law is neither good nor bad, simply irrelevant.
options.note: Paul insists the law is positively good, not neutral.

options.text: The law is good only for unbelievers.
options.note: The law does expose sin, but Paul insists it is good in itself for all, not merely useful for one audience.

quiz.q: How did sin use the good commandment, according to Paul?

options.text: Sin seized the commandment as an opportunity and produced covetous desire.
options.note: Sin abuses the good law, provoking the very desire it forbids.

options.text: Sin obeyed the commandment and became good.
options.note: Sin does not obey; it exploits the commandment against us.

options.text: Sin ignored the commandment entirely.
options.note: Sin did not ignore it; it seized it as a base of operations.

options.text: Sin was created by the commandment.
options.note: The commandment reveals and provokes sin, but does not create it; sin seizes the law as its opportunity.

quiz.q: What does the struggle I do not do the good I want reveal?

options.text: The reality of indwelling sin warring against the desire to do good.
options.note: The divided self shows sin still lodged in the flesh.

options.text: That the desire to do good is enough to guarantee doing it.
options.note: There is a real desire for good, but the passage shows desire alone does not guarantee the deed, for indwelling sin frustrates it.

options.text: That doing good is actually easy for everyone.
options.note: The passage shows the opposite, the frustration of the will by sin.

options.text: That the law is to blame for the failure.
options.note: Paul blames indwelling sin, not the law, which he calls good.

quiz.q: In the historic Reformed reading, who is the I of Romans 7:14 to 25?

options.text: The regenerate believer in ongoing struggle with indwelling sin.
options.note: Augustine, Luther, and Calvin read the delight in God's law and hatred of sin as marks of a renewed heart.

options.text: Paul under the law before Christ, or the unregenerate person.
options.note: A serious and fairly held view that stresses defeat under the law, though the historic reading foregrounds the believer's struggle.

options.text: Israel or Adam as a purely rhetorical everyman.
options.note: A representative reading exists, but it tends to loosen the personal force the passage carries.

options.text: A sinless person with no real battle against sin.
options.note: The passage is defined by struggle; it cannot describe a sinless life.

quiz.q: How is the cry, who will rescue me from this body of death, answered?

options.text: Thanks be to God, through Jesus Christ our Lord.
options.note: Deliverance comes not from self effort but from God through Christ.

options.text: By trying harder to keep the law.
options.note: This is the natural instinct, but the passage shows law keeping cannot deliver; rescue is through Christ alone.

options.text: By concluding that no rescue is possible.
options.note: The cry gives way at once to thanksgiving for real deliverance.

options.text: By escaping the body through mystical experience.
options.note: Deliverance is through Jesus Christ, not through escaping the body by mysticism.

questions: Paul insists the law is holy, righteous, and good, yet it cannot give the power to obey. How does this change what you expect from rules and resolutions in your own life?
Sin seizes even good commandments as an opportunity. Where have you seen a good boundary actually provoke the very desire it forbids in your heart?
The believer delights in God's law in the inner being yet still battles indwelling sin. How does it comfort you to know that this struggle is normal to the Christian life?
Paul cries wretched man that I am before giving thanks to God. Why is honest lament over remaining sin a healthy part of the Christian walk rather than a sign of failure?
The rescue comes through Jesus Christ our Lord, not through self effort. Where are you tempted to fight sin by willpower alone rather than looking to Christ and the Spirit?

translationNotes.note: Once I was alive apart from the law is variously understood, whether of Paul's own experience, of humanity in Adam, or of Israel before Sinai; it describes a former state of seeming life before the commandment brought the knowledge and power of sin to light.

Nothing good lives in me, that is, in my flesh, uses sarx for fallen human nature; Paul qualifies the statement with that is, in my flesh, since he also speaks of an inner being that delights in God's law.

The verse contains a line break in the source, rendered here as a single space: the thanksgiving through Jesus Christ our Lord is followed by the summary that with the mind Paul serves God's law but with the flesh the law of sin.

Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
